questions.q: Judicial review is the power of the courts to examine the validity of laws against which standard?
questions.explanation: Judicial review tests the constitutional validity of laws and executive actions.

questions.q: Which courts in India can exercise the power of judicial review?
options: The Supreme Court and the High Courts
questions.explanation: Both the Supreme Court and the High Courts exercise the power of judicial review.

questions.q: If a law is found to violate the Constitution, the courts may:
options: Refer it back to the public
questions.explanation: Courts can declare a law unconstitutional and strike it down to the extent of the violation.

questions.q: Judicial review primarily protects which part of the Constitution from infringement by laws?
questions.explanation: A key purpose of judicial review is to protect Fundamental Rights from legislative or executive violation.

questions.q: The Supreme Court is often described as the guardian of the:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court is regarded as the guardian and final interpreter of the Constitution.

questions.q: Article 13 declares that laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights shall be:
options: Void to the extent of inconsistency
questions.explanation: Article 13 provides that laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights are void to the extent of the inconsistency.

questions.q: Judicial review applies to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Judicial review extends to both legislation and executive or administrative actions.

questions.q: The writs that High Courts and the Supreme Court issue to enforce rights are a tool of:
questions.explanation: Writ jurisdiction enables courts to review and remedy violations through judicial review.

questions.q: Which article gives the Supreme Court power to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 32 empowers the Supreme Court to issue writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

questions.q: Which article empowers the High Courts to issue writs?
questions.explanation: Article 226 confers writ jurisdiction on the High Courts, with a wider scope than Article 32.

questions.q: The 1973 case that established the basic structure doctrine, limiting amendments through judicial review, was:
options: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati (1973) held that Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution.

questions.q: The 1967 case that initially held Fundamental Rights could not be amended by Parliament was:
options: Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
Sajjan Singh v. State of Rajasthan
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
questions.explanation: In Golak Nath (1967), the Supreme Court ruled that Parliament could not curtail Fundamental Rights through amendment.

questions.q: Which 1980 case struck down parts of the 42nd Amendment and reaffirmed judicial review as part of the basic structure?
options: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
questions.explanation: Minerva Mills (1980) held that judicial review and limited amending power are part of the basic structure.

questions.q: The concept of judicial review in modern constitutions is often traced to which landmark foreign judgment?
questions.explanation: Marbury v. Madison (1803) of the United States is regarded as the origin of modern judicial review.

questions.q: Public Interest Litigation, which expanded access to judicial review, developed prominently in India from which decade?
questions.explanation: Public Interest Litigation expanded the reach of judicial review beginning in the late 1970s and 1980s.

questions.q: Which writ is used to challenge the legality of a person's detention?
questions.explanation: The writ of habeas corpus is issued to test the legality of a person's detention.

questions.q: Which writ commands a public authority to perform a duty it has failed to perform?
questions.explanation: The writ of mandamus directs a public authority to perform a legal duty it has refused to perform.

questions.q: Which writ quashes an order already passed by a lower court or tribunal?
questions.explanation: The writ of certiorari quashes an order passed by a lower court or tribunal acting beyond its jurisdiction.

questions.q: Which writ questions the legal authority of a person to hold a public office?
questions.explanation: The writ of quo warranto challenges a person's legal authority to hold a public office.

questions.q: The Supreme Court's power to grant special leave to appeal from any court or tribunal flows from which article?
questions.explanation: Article 136 grants the Supreme Court discretionary power to grant special leave to appeal.

questions.q: Judicial review of constitutional amendments is most directly grounded in the doctrine established in which case?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati established that even amendments are subject to judicial review against the basic structure.

questions.q: The provision empowering the President to seek the Supreme Court's advisory opinion is which article?
questions.explanation: Article 143 allows the President to refer questions of law to the Supreme Court for an advisory opinion.

questions.q: In Minerva Mills, the Court struck down a clause that had tried to bar judicial review of amendments. That clause was inserted by which amendment?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment's clauses limiting judicial review of amendments were struck down in Minerva Mills (1980).

questions.q: The 2015 judgment striking down the National Judicial Appointments Commission as violative of the basic structure was in which case?
options: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association v. Union of India
questions.explanation: In the 2015 NJAC case, the Supreme Court struck down the 99th Amendment and the NJAC Act.

questions.q: The 2007 case holding that laws placed in the Ninth Schedule after 1973 are open to judicial review was:
options: I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
Waman Rao v. Union of India
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
questions.explanation: I.R. Coelho (2007) held that Ninth Schedule laws added after 24 April 1973 are subject to judicial review.

questions.q: The 24th Amendment, which sought to affirm Parliament's power to amend any part of the Constitution, was examined and limited in which case?
options: Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati upheld the 24th Amendment but subjected the amending power to the basic structure limit.

questions.q: The 1950 case that first interpreted the scope of 'procedure established by law' under Article 21 was:
options: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
Kharak Singh v. State of U.P.
A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
questions.explanation: A.K. Gopalan (1950) was among the earliest cases interpreting personal liberty under Article 21.

questions.q: The 1978 judgment that broadened Article 21 to require a 'just, fair and reasonable' procedure was:
options: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
questions.explanation: Maneka Gandhi (1978) expanded the interpretation of Article 21 through judicial review.

questions.q: The doctrine that an unconstitutional law is only void to the extent of its inconsistency, leaving the rest valid, is called:
options: The doctrine of pith and substance
questions.explanation: The doctrine of severability allows courts to strike only the invalid portion of a law where separable.

questions.q: The principle that a law valid at enactment but later inconsistent with rights becomes dormant rather than dead is the doctrine of:
questions.explanation: The doctrine of eclipse holds that a pre-constitutional law inconsistent with rights becomes dormant but revivable.
